title: Proxmox VE Cluster Quorum Loss Detection from Corosync and pmxcfs Signals
dataSources: `index=pve` `sourcetype="proxmox:task"` (pmxcfs, cluster join) and `sourcetype="proxmox:ha"` with corosync/pmxcfs keywords; optional dedicated `sourcetype="linux_syslog"` host corosync file
app: Splunk Universal Forwarder with inputs on `/var/log/corosync/corosync.log` and `journalctl -u corosync`; tag or merge into `proxmox:task` patterns
spl:
index=pve (sourcetype="proxmox:task" OR sourcetype="proxmox:ha") earliest=-4h
| search match(lower(_raw), "(?i)quorum|qdevice|not quorate|lost.*quorum|split.?brain|totem.*fault|ring.*fault")
| eval sev=if(match(lower(_raw), "(?i)lost.*quorum|not quorate|split.?brain"), "critical", "warning")
| bin _time span=5m
| stats count as sigs, values(host) as nodes by sev, _time
| where sigs>=5 AND sev="critical"
description: Quorum loss freezes cluster management APIs and can strand operators during incidents. Corosync and pmxcfs messages are the earliest observable signals of ring faults, vote drift, or qdevice misconfiguration.
value: Shortens time-to-detect for cluster brain-split conditions and prevents destructive manual intervention on partially connected nodes.
implementation: Route corosync logs with host, ring, and node identity. Build eventtypes for quorum phrases. Page on critical phrases; throttle warnings with a sustained-count threshold. Correlate with NIC driver updates and switch port events.
visualization: Timeline of quorum-related signals; choropleth or table of affected nodes; raw drilldown.
grandmaExplanation: We listen for signs that the servers lost agreement on who is in charge, which is when automatic fixes can accidentally make things worse unless people step in carefully.
knownFalsePositives: Nutanix Prism alerts and jobs can line up with disk rebuilds, rolling upgrades, or RF changes; wait for the cluster task list to show completion before over-reacting to transient counters.
